jokes.text: He's wonderful. I mean, he's hung like a field mouse.
jokes.setup: Guest asked to tell us more about his baby
jokes.punchline: Unexpected crude comparison of baby's anatomy to a field mouse
jokes.explanation: Classic Robin Williams improv - unexpected crude humor that feels spontaneous and character-appropriate for his manic persona

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: He's wonderful. I mean, he's hung like a field mouse.

jokes.text: I mean, you pop the diaper and... boing! It's like a little sundial.
jokes.setup: Building on the field mouse comparison
jokes.punchline: Escalates to sundial metaphor with physical gesture
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that transforms crude humor into absurdist poetry - the sundial metaphor is genuinely creative

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: I mean, you pop the diaper and... boing! It's like a little sundial.

jokes.text: Honey, what time is it? Get the baby. Get the baby. Look! It's 3:00! Oh, it's 2:30 now. It's real cold.
jokes.punchline: Full commitment to the bit with temperature affecting time
jokes.explanation: Peak Robin Williams - takes absurd premise to its logical conclusion with perfect commitment and timing

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: Honey, what time is it? Get the baby. Get the baby. Look! It's 3:00! Oh, it's 2:30 now. It's real cold.

jokes.text: It is like kryptonite for... for the father. Even the dog looks at me like, 'you don't put his face in it.'
jokes.setup: Baby poop is kryptonite for fathers
jokes.punchline: Even the dog judges his parenting incompetence
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor with good escalation from superhero reference to dog judgment

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: It is like kryptonite for... for the father. Even the dog looks at me like, 'you don't put his face in it.'

jokes.text: It's almost like I know that he's growing old because he's, like, he's 8 months and he has his own little credit card called 'fetal express.'
jokes.punchline: American Express becomes 'fetal express' credit card
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that's immediately graspable and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: It's almost like I know that he's growing old because he's, like, he's 8 months and he has his own little credit card called 'fetal express.'

jokes.text: He's dialing up one of those '976-suckle,' you know? I'm waiting for you. I'm lactating. Call me.
jokes.punchline: 976 phone sex lines become '976-suckle' for breastfeeding
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of 976 phone lines into nursing context - perfectly crafted and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: He's dialing up one of those '976-suckle,' you know? I'm waiting for you. I'm lactating. Call me.

jokes.text: I think I like the kryptonite suit. That's actually what it seems like.
jokes.punchline: Larry's flat callback attempt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders character moment - awkward host trying to connect with guest's energy

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I think I like the kryptonite suit. That's actually what it seems like.

jokes.text: What is it, is it Japanese? It is. And what is this? This is sears, right? Yes, so they're both Japanese. OK.
jokes.punchline: Completely nonsensical conclusion that Sears is Japanese
jokes.explanation: Pure Larry Sanders cringe - the host's desperate attempt at conversation creating awkward confusion

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: What is it, is it Japanese?
It is.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: And what is this?
This is sears, right?

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Yes, so they're both Japanese.
OK.

jokes.text: You hear that? That's my whippoorwill, Artie.
jokes.setup: Larry proudly identifying a bird sound
jokes.punchline: Sets up for Artie's correction
jokes.explanation: Good setup showing Larry's delusional nature about his Montana experience

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You hear that? That's my whippoorwill, Artie.

jokes.text: Oh, Larry, it's a magpie. They're scavengers, you know? They feed on road kill, dead meat. You know they carry rabies and lyme disease? You better not take a nap out on your porch. They'll peck your eyes out.
jokes.setup: Larry's romantic view of his whippoorwill
jokes.punchline: Artie's brutal reality check about dangerous scavenger birds
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie moment - brutally practical deflation of Larry's romantic delusions with escalating horror

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, Larry, it's a magpie. They're scavengers, you know? They feed on road kill, dead meat. You know they carry rabies and lyme disease? You better not take a nap out on your porch. They'll peck your eyes out.

jokes.text: Still. Every day.
jokes.punchline: Larry's stubborn refusal to accept reality
jokes.explanation: Masterful character comedy - Larry's delusional persistence in two words captures his entire psychology

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Still. Every day.

jokes.text: You're like one of those goddamn creatures out of Greek mythology... half man, half desk.
jokes.setup: Talk show animal like Greek mythology creature
jokes.punchline: Half man, half desk instead of centaur
jokes.explanation: Brilliant original metaphor that perfectly captures Larry's essence - highly quotable and character-specific

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You're like one of those goddamn creatures out of Greek mythology... half man, half desk.

jokes.text: Get off the porch, asshole.
jokes.punchline: Abrupt shift to yelling at an animal
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that breaks emotional moment with mundane reality

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Get off the porch, asshole.

jokes.text: Or we could say you had a drug problem. You had to hide out in montana till you dried out. I tell you, that's the only surefire way I know of avoiding embarrassment.
jokes.punchline: Drug problem is the least embarrassing option
jokes.explanation: Dark Hollywood satire - the twisted logic that drug addiction is preferable to other scandals

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Or we could say you had a drug problem. You had to hide out in montana till you dried out. I tell you, that's the only surefire way I know of avoiding embarrassment.

jokes.text: That's pathetic. We should save that one for when I really have a drug problem, don't you think?
jokes.punchline: Larry's practical consideration of future scandals
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry logic - accepting the inevitability of future scandals while being strategic about cover stories

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's pathetic. We should save that one for when I really have a drug problem, don't you think?

jokes.text: I love these fuckers.
jokes.punchline: Larry's perverse affection for those screwing him over
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's twisted relationship with the business

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I love these fuckers.

jokes.text: They don't even give you those little towels, those little wet towels. I had to wipe my face with a pack of sweet'n low.
jokes.punchline: Using artificial sweetener packets as wet towels
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy image and character-appropriate complaint from privileged Artie

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: They don't even give you those little towels, those little wet towels. I had to wipe my face with a pack of sweet'n low.

jokes.text: Man, I can't believe Leno actually hid in the closet so he could hear the whole network meeting. That's a sickness, to be so obsessed with what people are saying about you.
jokes.setup: Criticizing Leno for being obsessed with what people say
jokes.punchline: Sets up Larry's immediate hypocrisy
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the immediate payoff of Larry's hypocrisy

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Man, I can't believe Leno actually hid in the closet so he could hear the whole network meeting. That's a sickness, to be so obsessed with what people are saying about you.

jokes.text: I know. That is a sickness. What did people say about me not being mentioned much in the book?
jokes.setup: Just condemned obsessing over what people say
jokes.punchline: Immediately asks what people are saying about him
jokes.explanation: Perfect character hypocrisy moment - Larry's complete lack of self-awareness delivered with perfect timing

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I know. That is a sickness. What did people say about me not being mentioned much in the book?

jokes.text: And by pesos I don't mean to imply that someone of hispanic descent is likely to win. Again.
jokes.punchline: Hank's awkward attempt to not seem racist that makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank Kingsley moment - well-meaning but tone-deaf comment that makes things worse

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: And by pesos I don't mean to imply that someone of hispanic descent is likely to win. Again.

jokes.text: 'Say now'? Say now. Well, he can't say 'hey, now.' That's the intellectual property of the Larry Sanders show.
jokes.setup: Hank saying 'say now' instead of his catchphrase
jokes.punchline: Petty legal concerns over a simple greeting
jokes.explanation: Good meta humor about show business and intellectual property obsession

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 'Say now'? Say now. Well, he can't say 'hey, now.' That's the intellectual property of the Larry Sanders show.

jokes.text: You really pulled the rug out from under me, Larry boy.
jokes.punchline: Hank's passive-aggressive guilt trip
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's needy, guilt-tripping nature

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You really pulled the rug out from under me, Larry boy.

jokes.text: You had it on 'passenger window lock.'
jokes.setup: Larry complaining car window doesn't work
jokes.punchline: Simple mechanical explanation
jokes.explanation: Mild situational humor showing Larry's incompetence with basic things

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You had it on 'passenger window lock.'

jokes.text: Is this what you call low profile? Two grown men sitting in a car full of smoke? Looks like a goddamn cheech and chong movie.
jokes.punchline: Visual looks exactly like stoner comedy
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy reference that audience can immediately picture

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Is this what you call low profile? Two grown men sitting in a car full of smoke? Looks like a goddamn cheech and chong movie.

jokes.text: A free scratcher ticket?
jokes.setup: What do the lottery people give Hank
jokes.punchline: Larry's dismissive guess at cheap reward
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's condescending attitude toward Hank's new job

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: A free scratcher ticket?

jokes.text: They give me respect.
jokes.punchline: Hank's earnest response about respect
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - his desperate need for validation making a simple job meaningful

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: They give me respect.

jokes.text: I know, but I'm trying to keep this very low profile, darlene.
jokes.setup: Larry standing in dark office building at night
jokes.punchline: Claiming this bizarre behavior is 'low profile'
jokes.explanation: Character humor showing Larry's skewed perception of what's normal or discreet

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm trying to keep this very low profile, darlene.

jokes.text: But you never fired me. I didn't? No. Your business manager gives me a paycheck every week.
jokes.setup: Larry's apologetic speech about wanting her back
jokes.punchline: She was never actually fired
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that deflates Larry's dramatic gesture with simple practical reality

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: But you never fired me.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I didn't?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: No. Your business manager gives me a paycheck every week.

jokes.text: So what have you been up to the last 3 months, Phil? Oh, jerking off. See you Monday.
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest, inappropriate answer delivered casually
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery that subverts social niceties with uncomfortable honesty

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: So what have you been up to the last 3 months, Phil?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Oh, jerking off. See you Monday.

jokes.text: I need to hear you promise me that. (Larry says nothing)
jokes.setup: Arthur asks for promise that Larry won't abandon him again
jokes.punchline: Larry's telling silence
jokes.explanation: Perfect awkward silence that reveals character - Larry can't make the promise Arthur needs

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: I need to hear you promise me that.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: (Larry says nothing)

jokes.text: Oh, God. It's good to know I'll never have to pull another set of balls like that again.
jokes.setup: Hank reflecting on leaving lottery job
jokes.punchline: Unintentional sexual innuendo about lottery balls
jokes.explanation: Mild accidental innuendo. Fits Hank's character but not particularly clever or original.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Oh, God. It's good to know I'll never have to pull another set of balls like that again.

jokes.text: They should be cut out of here like cancer.
jokes.setup: Getting rid of useless staff members
jokes.punchline: Extreme medical metaphor for firing people
jokes.explanation: Good Artie character moment - his ruthless business mentality expressed through vivid metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: They should be cut out of here like cancer.

jokes.text: Larry walked, I put on back-to-back cheers reruns in the spot and they're doing rather well. Better then Leno as a matter of fact.
jokes.punchline: Reruns are beating current late night competition
jokes.explanation: Good industry satire - the brutal efficiency of television programming decisions

dialogue.speaker: Germain
dialogue.line: Larry walked, I put on back-to-back cheers reruns in the spot and they're doing rather well. Better then Leno as a matter of fact.

jokes.text: If you say you're gonna sue somebody, the least you can do is follow through and sue them.
jokes.setup: Network not following through on lawsuit
jokes.punchline: Artie's twisted sense of professional integrity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie logic - applying principles of integrity to completely amoral business practices

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If you say you're gonna sue somebody, the least you can do is follow through and sue them.

jokes.text: Oh, please, don't tell me you and Larry are lovers.
jokes.setup: Artie about to reveal a secret
jokes.punchline: Germain's immediate assumption it's a gay relationship
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that plays on Hollywood stereotypes and Germain's cynical assumptions

dialogue.speaker: Germain
dialogue.line: Oh, please, don't tell me you and Larry are lovers.

jokes.text: Something about the dynamic?
jokes.setup: Artie's shocked reaction to lovers suggestion
jokes.punchline: Germain's analytical observation of their relationship
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Germain's detached, analytical view of relationships

dialogue.speaker: Germain
dialogue.line: Something about the dynamic?

jokes.text: Well, that explains a lot... the mood swings, the attention disorder, the astonishing weight gain.
jokes.setup: Learning about Larry's supposed drug problem
jokes.punchline: Retroactively explaining Larry's behavior with increasingly harsh observations
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of insults disguised as understanding, ending with the brutal weight gain comment

dialogue.speaker: Germain
dialogue.line: Well, that explains a lot... the mood swings, the attention disorder, the astonishing weight gain.

jokes.text: He's as clean as Louie Anderson's dinner plate.
jokes.punchline: Reference to heavy comedian's eating habits
jokes.explanation: Clever metaphor that works on multiple levels - both about cleanliness and comedy insider knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: He's as clean as Louie Anderson's dinner plate.

jokes.text: I didn't hear it ring. Oh, it didn't. It's a new system. You see, there's a little flashing light here you can't see from where you're standing.
jokes.setup: Artie needs to interrupt private conversation
jokes.punchline: Elaborate lie about invisible phone system
jokes.explanation: Good Artie improvisation under pressure. The specificity of the invisible light is perfect character touch.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I didn't hear it ring. Oh, it didn't. It's a new system. You see, there's a little flashing light here you can't see from where you're standing.

jokes.text: Where's the gent's? Uh, right out the door, first crapper on the left.
jokes.punchline: Artie's crude response to executive's refined language
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast showing Artie's working-class directness vs executive politeness

dialogue.speaker: Germain
dialogue.line: Where's the gent's?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Uh, right out the door, first crapper on the left.

jokes.text: What'd he mean by astonishing weight gain?
jokes.setup: Germain's earlier insult about Larry's weight
jokes.punchline: Larry fixating on the personal insult
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Larry's vanity and tendency to focus on personal slights

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What'd he mean by astonishing weight gain?

jokes.text: Never mind that. What about the gay lovers thing?
jokes.punchline: Artie more concerned about gay assumption
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing different character priorities and insecurities

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Never mind that. What about the gay lovers thing?

jokes.text: ¡vamanos! ¡andalay! ¡pronto!
jokes.punchline: Artie's random Spanish exclamations
jokes.explanation: Mild character comedy showing Artie's tendency toward dramatic flourishes

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: ¡vamanos! ¡andalay! ¡pronto!

jokes.text: He hasn't changed.
jokes.setup: Larry's needy behavior about the song
jokes.punchline: Bernadette's dry observation to someone else
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with economy - Bernadette's assessment of Larry's character

dialogue.line: He hasn't changed.

jokes.text: I had a... ahem... an addiction to painkillers.
jokes.punchline: Forced public confession on live TV
jokes.explanation: Peak cringe moment as Larry has to publicly lie about drug addiction for his career

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I had a... ahem... an addiction to painkillers.

jokes.text: Yeah, she got that because that's how they're wearing it in prison.
jokes.punchline: Prediction about her legal troubles
jokes.explanation: Decent topical humor that works as both fashion observation and political commentary

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah, she got that because that's how they're wearing it in prison.

jokes.text: I actually saw a hooker dressed in a barney outfit. It was unbelievable. Now when barney sings, 'you love me, I love you,' it costs you 250 bucks.
jokes.punchline: Hookers adapting to family-friendly environment with children's character costumes
jokes.explanation: Strong absurdist premise that logically follows from the setup with good escalation to price point

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I actually saw a hooker dressed in a barney outfit. It was unbelievable. Now when barney sings, 'you love me, I love you,' it costs you 250 bucks.

jokes.text: Larry comes out and he tells the people that he was molested as a child. All right? Of course, I mean we have people who call your parents and we tell them we're just making it up.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of calling parents to explain it's fake
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy that captures the amoral nature of television programming with perfect escalation

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Larry comes out and he tells the people that he was molested as a child. All right? Of course, I mean we have people who call your parents and we tell them we're just making it up.

jokes.text: And every night, I say a prayer, thank the lord, Hank Kingsley's back!
jokes.punchline: Reveals the 'friend' is actually Hank, not Larry
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - entire emotional poem was about Hank, not Larry. Perfect structure building to devastating reveal that deflates Larry's ego completely.

dialogue.speaker: Bernadette Peters
dialogue.line: And every night, I say a prayer, thank the lord, Hank Kingsley's back!

jokes.text: Thank the lord, Hank Kingsley's back!
jokes.punchline: Reveals the poem was actually about himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank Kingsley moment - what seemed like genuine sentiment revealed as pure narcissism with perfect execution

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Thank the lord, Hank Kingsley's back!
